claim: The Transformer paper targeted sequence transduction without relying on recurrent neural networks.
Scaled dot-product attention compares queries and keys, then uses the resulting weights to combine values.
Q, K, and V are projected representations used by attention to decide what information each token should read.
Self-attention allows the model to process token relationships without the sequential recurrence bottleneck.
The important shift was not only model size, but the way the architecture reads relationships across a sequence.
Transformer-style architectures became foundational for many later large language models.
A practical explanation of the paper can be organized around the problem, attention mechanism, and later impact.
